**MySQL database setup**

On MySQL, we have created a database with four tables inside it, the ‘toll’ table will be used for storing summarized data of what is stored on mongoDB, while we have proposed the ‘alerts’ table as a proof of concept, an idea we would develop on a future project, a suggestion rather than a functional part; a table that registers alerts of inoperative booths per area on the last 15 minutes. As for the other two tables, ‘area’ and ‘vehicles’, they will be used for storing the names a number on the attributes tollArea(toll,alerts) and vehicleType(toll) is representing. So this is the data we collect on each one:

Toll:

* day: It stores the date on YYYY-MM-DD format, as the datatype is DATE.  
* hour: This stores the hour as a number.  
* tollArea: This stores the number that represents an area.  
* booth: It stores a number that represents a booth.  
* vehicleType: It stores a number that represents a vehicle type.  
* countPerVehicleType: It is the sum of all the vehicles that have been registered on a certain booth in a certain area per vehicle type and hour of the day, so it is a number.  
* totalVehiclesInHour: It is the sum of all registered vehicles of a given hour on a certain day, so it is a number.

Alerts:

* booth: It represents the booth number.  
* lastActivity: It saves the registered last activity, as YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss format (It is a datetime type).  
* tollArea: It saves the number of the toll.

Area:

* areaId: The area number.  
* areaName: The area name areaId makes reference to, saved as varchar(50), so that it can store up to 50 symbols.

Vehicle:

* vehicleId: The vehicle number.  
* type: The vehicle name the previous column makes reference to, saved as varchar(50), so that it can store up to 50 symbols.

\*When we say a column value is stored as a number, we refer that it is stored as an int.

These are the commands we have used for creating our database:

create database toll3;  
use toll3;

create table toll (  
    day date not null,  
    hour int not null,  
    tollArea int not null,  
    booth int not null,  
    vehicleType int not null,  
    countPerVehicleType int not null,  
    totalVehiclesInHour int not null,  
    PRIMARY KEY (day, hour, booth, vehicleType)  
);  
\*We have added the PK to day, hour, booth and vehicleType because the information will be classified per all those attributes.

create table alerts (  
    tollArea int not null,  
    booth int not null,  
    lastActivity datetime not null,  
    PRIMARY KEY (tollArea, booth)  
);  
\*We have put PK to tollArea and booth because the booth depends on area.

create table area (  
areaId int,  
areaName varchar(50) not null,  
constraint area\_pk primary key (areaId)  
);  
create table vehicle (  
vehicleId int,  
type varchar(50) not null,  
constraint vehicle\_pk primary key (vehicleId)  
);  
\*This two tables have respectively areaId and vehicleId as PK, as these tables serve as ‘indexes’ for their respective area.

When creating the tables, we have used ‘not null’ constraints in all attributes on all tables (except on the PKs of tables with single attribute PK, as those can not be null anyway), as every attribute must be entered.

Then we have altered the tables in order to relate tables to each other by adding FKs. Apart from that, we have also added an ‘on update cascade’ line on each one so that if the areaId and vehicleId is changed on their respective tables, it is automatically updated on the other tables:

alter table toll  
add constraint tollArea\_toll\_fk  
foreign key (tollArea)  
references area(areaId)  
on update cascade;

alter table toll  
add constraint vehicleType\_fk  
foreign key (vehicleType)  
references vehicle(vehicleId)  
on update cascade;

alter table alerts  
add constraint tollArea\_alerts\_fk  
foreign key (tollArea)  
references area(areaId)  
on update cascade;

![](db1.png)

Then we have added data on tables vehicle and area, so that the data that depends on it on the other 2 tables can be inserted through the automated script:

insert into vehicle (vehicleId, type) values (1, "car");  
insert into vehicle (vehicleId, type) values (2, "truck");  
insert into vehicle (vehicleId, type) values (3, "bus");  
insert into vehicle (vehicleId, type) values (4, "motor");

insert into area (areaId, areaName) values (1, "itziar");  
insert into area (areaId, areaName) values (2, "ermua");  
insert into area (areaId, areaName) values (3, "durango");

This is how our database looks like after entering this data and then letting the script that inserts data onto it run for a while:  
![](db2.png)

**inserting data MongoDB to MariaDB**

For our script that enters data from mongodb into mysql to function properly, we had to start by installing all required packages (node itself and the dependencies our script could have) on the folder where our script is, so we executed these commands on that location using cmd as admin mode (we have installed also packages needed by python):  
    • npm init \-y  
    • npm install mongodb json2csv mariadb mysql2 node-cron  
    • pip install mysql-connector-python pymongo pandas

As the script requires the Apache and MySQL services to be opened for functioning, we had to figure out how to launch those services on boot:

![](db3.png) 
![](db4.png)
![](db5.png) 
![](db6.png)

This way apache and mySQL will start on boot:  
![](db7.png);

After figuring this, we started researching how we could convert our script into autoexecutable. We came with the idea of using PM2 (Process Manager 2), a process manager specifically designed for [node.js](http://node.js), because contrary to Windows Scheduler, another option we considered, it fulfills way more simpler our goal; as we have choosen several frequencies data will be transferred between databases, depending on the hour.

Contrary to Windows Scheduler, PM2 allows us to put directly the frequency data will be transferred inside our script itself; as PM2 is capable of ‘awakening’ our script at the right time.

Now, to make our script autoexecutable, we have to launch this command where our script is located:

(firstly we install pm2: npm install pm2 \-g)  
pm2 start [bbblaburpenaOrduroXminitturikBehin.js](http://bbblaburpenaOrduroXminitturikBehin.js)  
![](db8.png)

pm2 save  
![](db9.png)

Then, by typing “shell:startup” on the file explorer, we will end on this folder, where we will have to create a vbs file so that our script starts automatically when the pc is switched on:

![](db10.png)  
![](db11.png)

After rebooting the pc, we can see the service gets started on its own:  
![](db12.png)

If we are changing the code, we will have to restart it so that changes are applied: pm2 restart bbblaburpenaOrduroXminitturikBehin

pm2 save

![](db13.png)

The script automates sending information of the counted vehicles per vehicle type, booth, the area and the day period.  
This is how our mySQL database looks like before automatising  the data transfer: We have let the program run so that on the same time period (first 2 columns for the date and hour of the day), we keep registering vehicles per area, booth and vehicle type.  
![](db14.png)

After letting the script run for a while:  
![](db15.png)
On the same script, we do also have a query that inserts rows on another table; registers of booths in an area that has not seen traffic for the last 15minutes. But as this is only a proof of concept, we are not using it, we have commented it.

This is the frequency data will be sent from mongodb to mysql:  
![](db16.png) 
As it can be seen on the picture, the script functions on a different frequency depending on the hour it is runned:

* On the 8:00-12:00 period the program is launched every 24 minutes. (it is not exactly every 24 minutes on this period due to that we have to send different values through it on one moment, breaking the frequency on which data is sent)  
* On the 12:00-14:00 period the program is launched every 15 minutes.  
* From the 14:00 to 8:00 period the program is launched only at 8o’clock in the morning.  
* In the alternative query’s case, it would be executed every 15 minutes, independently of the time period.

We have decided to launch the script with this frequency taking into account how much time does mongodb get around 1000 documents’ worth of new data.

**sql queries**

The data that is inserted into MySQL will have different purposes:

* We will export all data from a given day on csv and json format for our website’s opendata section (the first query, the general query).  
* We will export processed data from a specific day in json format for showing it as a graph on our website (the second query).  
* Other queries that could in the future be shown on our web page

Our sql queries:  
    • the general query, where we see everything it is stored on our toll table:  
      SELECT toll.day, toll.hour, area.areaName, toll.booth, vehicle.type, toll.countPerVehicleType, toll.totalVehiclesInHour FROM toll INNER JOIN area ON toll.tollArea \= area.areaId INNER JOIN vehicle ON toll.vehicleType \= vehicle.vehicleId ORDER BY toll.day, toll.hour, toll.booth, vehicle.type;

    • the general query but shown with percentages instead instead of showing the counts:  
      SELECT toll.day, toll.hour, area.areaName, toll.booth, vehicle.type, (toll.countPerVehicleType \* 100.0 / toll.totalVehiclesInHour) AS vehiclePercentage FROM toll INNER JOIN area ON toll.tollArea \= area.areaId INNER JOIN vehicle ON toll.vehicleType \= vehicle.vehicleId ORDER BY toll.day, toll.hour, toll.booth, vehicle.type;

    • vehicles per area:  
      SELECT area.areaName, SUM(toll.countPerVehicleType) as total FROM toll INNER JOIN area ON toll.tollArea \= area.areaId GROUP BY area.areaName;

    • vehicles per area on a given day:  
      SELECT area.areaName, SUM(toll.countPerVehicleType) as total FROM toll INNER JOIN area ON toll.tollArea \= area.areaId WHERE toll.day \>= '2026-04-22' AND toll.day \<= '2026-04-23' GROUP BY area.areaName;

    • inactive booths on the last 15 minutes:  
      SELECT  alerts.booth,  area.areaName,  alerts.lastActivity FROM alerts INNER JOIN area ON alerts.tollArea \= area.areaId WHERE alerts.lastActivity \< NOW() \- INTERVAL 15 MINUTE;

    • vehicles per booth:  
      SELECT  toll.booth,  area.areaName, SUM(toll.countPerVehicleType) as total FROM toll  INNER JOIN area ON toll.tollArea \= area.areaId GROUP BY toll.booth, area.areaName ORDER BY area.areaName ASC;

    • vehicles per booth on a given day:  
      SELECT toll.booth, area.areaName, SUM(toll.countPerVehicleType) as total FROM toll INNER JOIN area ON toll.tollArea \= area.areaId WHERE toll.day \>= '2026-04-22' AND toll.day \<= '2026-04-23' GROUP BY toll.booth, area.areaName ORDER BY area.areaName ASC;

**mongodb queries**

This is one version of the query we use in the script, in there we use it with a match (something like this:  
$match: {  
                    timestamp: { $gte: hasieraData, $lte: orain }  
                }  
), as if we do not use it, mysql database is fed with repeated data:  
db.toll.aggregate(\[  
  {  
    $group: {  
      \_id: {  
        day: { $dateToString: { format: "%Y-%m-%d", date: "$timestamp" } },  
        hour: { $hour: "$timestamp" },  
        area: "$tollArea",  
        tollBooth: "$tollBooth",  
        vehicleType: "$vehicleType"  
      },  
      count: { $sum: 1 }  
    }  
  },  
  {  
    $setWindowFields: {  
      partitionBy: {  
        day: "$\_id.day",  
        hour: "$\_id.hour",  
        area: "$\_id.area",  
        tollBooth: "$\_id.tollBooth"  
      },  
      output: {  
        totalVehiclesInHour: { $sum: "$count" }  
      }  
    }  
  },  
  {  
    $project: {  
      \_id: 0,  
      day: "$\_id.day",  
      hour: "$\_id.hour",  
      area: "$\_id.area",  
      tollBooth: "$\_id.tollBooth",  
      vehicleType: "$\_id.vehicleType",  
      count: "$count",  
      totalVehiclesInHour: 1  
    }  
  }  
\])

This query calculates how many vehicles of each type pass through each toll booth of each area per hour, and also the total vehicles in that same hour (all types combined). To do so, we start by grouping the vehicles by the mentioned attributes, and also dividing the timestamp into what we are interested. We count all that and then with setWindowField, we count all the previously calculated (individually) ‘situation’ (counts per area, booth and vehicle for an hour of a day) and adds them i nt totalVehiclesInHour. Then with project we select all we want to show.

![](db17.png)

We also have a version of an alternative query we could integrate on our project: This query finds the most recent activity for each toll booth by sorting the results from the most recent to the oldest (as we are using timestamp):  
db.toll.aggregate(\[  
  { $sort: { timestamp: \-1 } },  
  {  
    $group: {  
      \_id: "$tollBooth",  
      lastActivity: { $first: "$timestamp" },  
      area: { $first: "$tollArea" }  
    }  
  }  
\])

This is the query that selects data for entering into our table alerts, but then on the tests we have run on the script, we have kept filtering the results with sql.

![](db18.png)

We also have this other query that we are using for getting the documents we will pass to csv and json format (used on mongodb\_tik\_json\_csv\_era\_js.js), on which the only thing we do is to match the timestamp to what we need:  
db.toll.aggregate(\[  
 {  
    $match: {  
      timestamp: {   
        $gte: ISODate("2026-02-25T00:00:00.000Z"),   
        $lte: ISODate("2026-02-25T23:59:59.999Z")   
      }  
    }  
  }  
\])

![](db19.png)

Apart from these three queries we have another queries we could use for seen interesting information on our mongodb database:

    • Average vehicle quantity on all areas:  
      db.toll.aggregate(\[  
        {  
          $group: {  
            \_id: "$tollBooth",  
            total: { $sum: 1 }  
          }  
        },  
        {  
          $group: {  
            \_id: null,  
            avgTolls: { $avg: "$total" }  
          }  
        }  
      \])

This query calculates the average vehicles registered on every booth, it groups the documents by booths and then count them. And then groups them again for then calculating the average with ‘total’.

    • Unwind used with paymentmethod  
      db.toll.aggregate(\[  
        {  
          $project: {  
            paymentArray: \["$paymentMethod"\]  
          }  
        },  
        { $unwind: "$paymentArray" },  
        {  
          $group: {  
            \_id: "$paymentArray",  
            total: { $sum: 1 }  
          }  
        }  
      \])

First with project parameter we get the payment method of each document, and then with unwind we take the payment method out of the array. Then with group we group the documents according to their payment method, and we count them.

    • peak hour per day  
      db.toll.aggregate(\[  
        {  
          $project: {  
            day: { $dateToString: { format: "%Y-%m-%d", date: "$timestamp" } },  
            hour: { $hour: "$timestamp" }  
          }  
        },  
        {  
          $group: {  
            \_id: { day: "$day", hour: "$hour" },  
            total: { $sum: 1 }  
          }  
        },  
        { $sort: { total: \-1 } }  
      \])

With project we start by extracting the day and hour out of the timestamp, and then we group the documents according to these attributes, and we count them. Then by using sort we organize them on descendent order using \-1.

    • Function for classifying by traffic level:  
      db.toll.aggregate(\[  
        {  
          $match: {  
            timestamp: {  
              $gte: ISODate("2026-04-22T07:00:00.000Z"),  
              $lt: ISODate("2026-04-22T08:00:00.000Z")  
            }  
          }  
        },  
        {  
          $group: {  
            \_id: "$tollArea",  
            total: { $sum: 1 }  
          }  
        },  
        {  
          $addFields: {  
            trafficLevel: {  
              $switch: {  
                branches: \[  
                  { case: { $gt: \["$total", 1000\] }, then:"HIGH" },  
                  { case: { $gt: \["$total", 500\] }, then:"MEDIUM" }  
                \],  
                default: "LOW"  
              }  
            }  
          }  
        }  
      \])

Here we start by matching the documents by a certain day’s hour period, and then we count the grouped by tollArea. Then we add a new ‘column’, ‘trafficLevel’, and inside it by using ‘switch’ we put a condition (as inside switch we have put branches, we can put more than one condition, as we have). we start the condition with case, and inside of it we have put ‘gt’ (greater than), so that if ‘total’ is greater than 1000, the trafficLevel field should show ‘HIGH’. On the second condition we have done more or less the same; but besides ‘branches’ we have also put ‘default’ inside the switch, which trafficLevel should show when the conditions inside ‘branches’ are not met.

    • the data regarding payment method: its count and percentage.  
      db.toll.aggregate(\[  
        {  
          $group: {  
            \_id: "$paymentMethod",  
            count: { $sum: 1 }  
          }  
        },  
        {  
          $group: {  
            \_id: null,  
            total: { $sum: "$count" },  
            methods: { $push: "$$ROOT" }  
          }  
        },  
        { $unwind: "$methods" },  
        {  
          $project: {  
            method: "$methods.\_id",  
            count: "$methods.count",  
            percentage: {  
              $multiply: \[  
                { $divide: \["$methods.count", "$total"\] },  
                100  
              \]  
            }  
          }  
        }  
      \])

We start by grouping and counting by payment method, and then we count the previous counts with the second count; with push: $$ROOT we also put all the fields (push adds another element to an array; $$ROOT= the entire document) of the first group inside methods, we put them into an array. Then with unwind we split the array into rows, and then with project we project what we dire to project. On this case, we have decided to show a field  ‘percentage’, which we obtain its value by multiplying by 100 what we get by firstly dividing ‘methods.count’ by ‘total’; but as we have decided to show percentage, we could as well think not to show the count; depending on our needs.

**automate tasks**

As we need to automate several things, and all share the need to be executed on a daily basis, we have created a task on Windows Scheduler that will be executed everyday at 8:10; a task that will execute a .bat archive that will itself execute other programs. This is how we have configured it:  
![](db20.png) 
![](db21.png)
![](db22.png) 
then on action ‘Iniciar un programa’

![](db23.png) 
In ‘programa o script’ we have put the path of the .bat file we have created for executing the tasks we are interested in, putting the path within “s. Then in ‘iniciar en’ we will put the folder’s path our .bat file is on, without using “s.

After creating the script we have finished configuring it the way we want by editing it so that the script is performed even if the pc is unplugged:  
![](db24.png)

If needed, we can also see if it is working:  
![](db25.png)
If we are unsure whether it is working or not, we can also execute it at any moment with ‘ejecutar’.

Regarding the .bat our task executes, this is what it does:  
@echo off  
F:  
cd "F:\\erronkak\\erronketako neure artxibuk \+ erronkan esplikaziñoko dokumentuk\\erronka3\\2parti"  
node biexport.js  
exit

Firstly it navigates to where the program we are automatizing is, for then executing it. Then [biexport.js](http://biexport.js) itself calls other .js scripts which exports data from our mongoDB database, as well as from our mySQL database into CSV and JSON files (the exported data is only regarding to all registers of the previous day). [biexport.js](http://biexport.js) also executes our complete mySQL database dump, as that is what we decided to ensure data is not lost.

This is how [biexport.js](http://biexport.js) looks like:  
![](db26.png) 
As it can be seen, it firstly executes the scripts that turn mongodb and mysql data into json and csv files, and then it gets today’s data for the sql database dump file’s name, and then it performs it giving it that name.

**sql dump**

The goal is to ensure no data is lost, as well as wrong able to reset data from any given day if something went wrong.  
To do so, our main strategy is to export our whole database in an automated way, with the script mentioned earlier,  so that we eliminate the possible human errors. We will import the database as a SQL file, as exporting this way guarantees it is easily manageable and portable.

export the database:  
cd C:\\xampp\\mysql\\bin  
mysqldump \-u root \--password="" toll2 \> "C:\\Users\\Peru\\Downloads\\toll2\_backup.sql"

![](db27.png)

Restore our database:  
mysql \-u root \--password="" \-e "CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS toll4;"  
mysql \-u root \--password="" toll4 \< "C:\\Users\\Peru\\Downloads\\toll2\_backup.sql"

Alternatively, we have decided that we could, instead of dumping the complete database, dump only its structure (the tables without their information; their PKs, other constraints, how they are related…). And then extract the tables information into CSV files. This is how we have done so:

Export the database structure:  
cd C:\\xampp\\mysql\\bin  
mysqldump \-u root \--no-data toll2 \> "C:\\Users\\Peru\\Downloads\\toll2\_scheme.sql"

for exporting data:  
mysql \-u root \-e "SELECT \* FROM area INTO OUTFILE 'C:/Users/Peru/Downloads/area.csv' FIELDS TERMINATED BY ';' ENCLOSED BY '\\"' LINES TERMINATED BY '\\n';" toll2

mysql \-u root \-e "SELECT \* FROM alerts INTO OUTFILE 'C:/Users/Peru/Downloads/alerts.csv' FIELDS TERMINATED BY ';' ENCLOSED BY '\\"' LINES TERMINATED BY '\\n';" toll2

mysql \-u root \-e "SELECT \* FROM toll INTO OUTFILE 'C:/Users/Peru/Downloads/toll.csv' FIELDS TERMINATED BY ';' ENCLOSED BY '\\"' LINES TERMINATED BY '\\n';" toll2

mysql \-u root \-e "SELECT \* FROM vehicle INTO OUTFILE 'C:/Users/Peru/Downloads/vehicle.csv' FIELDS TERMINATED BY ';' ENCLOSED BY '\\"' LINES TERMINATED BY '\\n';" toll2

![](db28.png)

Restore our database:

![](db29.png)  
on cmd:  
![](db30.png)
C:\\xampp\\mysql\\bin\\mysql.exe \-u root \-e "CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS toll3;"

C:\\xampp\\mysql\\bin\\mysql.exe \-u root toll3 \< "C:\\Users\\Peru\\Downloads\\toll2\_scheme.sql"

C:\\xampp\\mysql\\bin\\mysql.exe \--local-infile=1 \-u root \-e "LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE 'C:\\\\Users\\\\Peru\\\\Downloads\\\\area.csv' INTO TABLE area FIELDS TERMINATED BY ';' ENCLOSED BY '\\"' LINES TERMINATED BY '\\n';" toll3

C:\\xampp\\mysql\\bin\\mysql.exe \--local-infile=1 \-u root \-e "LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE 'C:\\\\Users\\\\Peru\\\\Downloads\\\\vehicle.csv' INTO TABLE vehicle FIELDS TERMINATED BY ';' ENCLOSED BY '\\"' LINES TERMINATED BY '\\n';" toll3

C:\\xampp\\mysql\\bin\\mysql.exe \--local-infile=1 \-u root \-e "LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE 'C:\\\\Users\\\\Peru\\\\Downloads\\\\toll.csv' INTO TABLE toll FIELDS TERMINATED BY ';' ENCLOSED BY '\\"' LINES TERMINATED BY '\\n';" toll3

C:\\xampp\\mysql\\bin\\mysql.exe \--local-infile=1 \-u root \-e "LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE 'C:\\\\Users\\\\Peru\\\\Downloads\\\\alerts.csv' INTO TABLE alerts FIELDS TERMINATED BY ';' ENCLOSED BY '\\"' LINES TERMINATED BY '\\n';" toll3

![](db31.png) 
![](db32.png)

This strategy, although very interesting in certain conditions, is not the one we will use; as human intervention is minimal, we should not have the necessity to interact with data. This strategy would be ideal on less automated conditions, therefore it would be ideal for environments that might be more prone to human errors.

**alter node-red function**

Analizing how much data enters during certain time periods, we have seen that the data input is kind of slow, that is why we have modified one of the node-red data generating functions, so that for example when the time period is from 8 to 12

This is how one of our data generating script on node-red program looks like:  
![](db33.png)
![](db34.png)
There, we can see we have the code which specifies when will the next vehicle be generated, as well as the attributes it will have: Whether if it has paid with a credit card, cash or viaT; the toll booth it has passed from (1/2/3), the areas as well (1/2/3/4)…

We would like to speed the frequency data is generated when it is not the period between 8 to 12, so we will let it like this, so that a new vehicle is generated by this script on a period between 100 and 200 milliseconds:  
minDelay \= 100; // 100 milliseconds  
maxDelay \= 200; // 200 milliseconds

We will also change this part, so that bitcoin is also included on the payment method:  
![](db35.png)

const paymentMethods \= \["credit card", "cash", "viaT", "bitcoin"\];

![](db36.png)

After doing the changes, we will see how fast data arrives:  
![](db37.png)
